title: Authorization check via tx.origin
description: tx.origin returns the EOA that started the transaction chain — using it for authorization lets an attacker phish a privileged user into calling a malicious contract that then forwards the call back to the protected function. Use msg.sender for caller-identity checks.
remediation: Replace tx.origin with msg.sender in any access-control modifier or require() check.

title: Block-state used as a randomness source
description: block.timestamp, block.number, block.difficulty, and block.prevrandao are all influenceable by miners or proposers within a small window. Using them to seed lotteries, NFT mint orders, or any value an attacker would profit from manipulating is unsafe.
remediation: Use a commit-reveal scheme, a VRF (e.g. Chainlink VRF), or an oracle for randomness. Document any deliberate use of block-state randomness with a // pencheff:allow comment.

title: Deprecated SELFDESTRUCT opcode
description: selfdestruct() was deprecated in Solidity 0.8.18; per EIP-6049 its behaviour is changing and the contract may not actually be removed from state on future hardforks. Existing patterns that rely on it for fund recovery / migration may silently break.
remediation: Migrate to an explicit pause/upgrade pattern (e.g. OpenZeppelin's UUPS) instead of relying on selfdestruct.

title: Low-level call return value discarded
description: address.call() / .delegatecall() / .staticcall() return a (bool success, bytes memory data) tuple. Discarding the success flag means a failed call silently succeeds at the caller — funds get stuck, state changes are lost. The classic foot-gun behind King-of-the-Hill exploits.
remediation: Always capture and check the success flag: `(bool ok, ) = target.call{value: amount}(""); require(ok, "call failed");`
